name: Utpaladeva
dates_notes: Dated by guru-paramparā with Somānanda and pre-Abhinavagupta.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II's Nareśvara-Parīkṣā-Prakāśa is the principal dualist Saiddhāntika reply to the Pratyabhijñā position founded by Somānanda and articulated by Utpaladeva.

Pratyabhijñā / Trika inherits the Tathāgatagarbha tradition's emphasis on prakṛti-prabhāsvara-citta (naturally luminous mind) into its own articulation of the always-already-Śiva nature of awareness. The relation is one of structural inheritance through the shared northern-Buddhist doctrinal milieu rather than direct citation; recent scholarship (Sanderson, Torella, Ratié) shows Utpaladeva's epistemological apparatus in sustained dialogue with the Buddhist Pramāṇa school, with the metaphysical inheritance from Tathāgatagarbha being more diffuse.

The Laṅkāvatāra-Sūtra's articulation of citta-mātra and of the cognitive ground of awakening is part of the Sanskrit-source background within which Utpaladeva's Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā operates; the sūtra's emphasis on the natural luminosity of the cognitive ground informs the Trika articulation of cit as svaprakāśa, even where direct citation is rare.

core_thesis: Utpaladeva is the founder of the *Pratyabhijñā* ("recognition") system, which articulates a non-dual idealism in which the sole reality is Śiva-as-consciousness (*saṃvit*, *cit*), whose intrinsic nature is *svātantrya* — absolute, unconditioned sovereign freedom ([*Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā* 1.5.13–14](cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.13-14), the *aham-vimarśa* analysis). *Svātantrya* is not a property added to consciousness; it *is* consciousness as self-luminous and self-aware (*prakāśa-vimarśa*-rūpā) ([*ĪPK* 1.5.10–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.10-17)). The whole manifest world — including the limited subject, its objects, time, and causality — is *ābhāsa*, Śiva's own appearing, freely projected within himself by his own *svātantrya* ([*ĪPK* 2.3.1–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.3.1-17) on *ābhāsa-vāda*; [*ĪPK* 1.5.7](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.7) on the world as *cid-ābhāsa*). The bound condition of the *paśu* therefore consists not in any real ontological fall but in non-recognition (*akhyāti*) of the supreme *I*-consciousness (*pūrṇāhantā*) that is in fact already and inalienably one's own nature ([*ĪPK* 1.1.3](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.1.3) on *akhyāti* as the structural condition of bondage; [*ĪPK* 4.1.1–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.1.1-17) on *pratyabhijñā* as the resolving recognition). Liberation accordingly is *pratyabhijñā*: not the production of a new state but the recognition that *one is already* Śiva ([*ĪPK* 4.1.16–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.1.16-17), the concluding *upasaṃhāra*). The corpus develops this through the technical apparatus of *ābhāsa-vāda*, *vimarśa*, and the *aham*-analysis. Compared to Vedānta this preserves a positive, dynamic agentive consciousness rather than a witnessing Brahman ([*ĪPK* 2.4.1–21](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.4.1-21) on *kriyā* as Śiva's own intrinsic *śakti*).

engaged_works.title: Ishvara-Pratyabhijna-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the founding text of the Pratyabhijñā school.
engaged_works.summary: The founding text of the Pratyabhijñā system, in roughly 190 verses divided into four *adhikāras* — *Jñāna* ([*ĪPK* 1](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1), eight *āhnikas*), *Kriyā* ([*ĪPK* 2](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2), four *āhnikas*), *Āgama* ([*ĪPK* 3](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3), two *āhnikas*), *Tattva-saṃgraha* ([*ĪPK* 4](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4), one *āhnika*) — with Utpaladeva's own *Vṛtti* (and the longer lost *Ṭīkā*). Building on Somānanda's *Śivadṛṣṭi*, the work argues that the supreme reality is *cit* (consciousness) characterized by intrinsic *vimarśa* — self-aware reflexivity — and intrinsic *svātantrya* ([*ĪPK* 1.5.10–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.10-17)). The *Jñāna-adhikāra* establishes the structure of cognition (*prakāśa*-*vimarśa*) and the necessity of a unitary self-aware subject ([*ĪPK* 1.1.1–5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.1.1-5); [*ĪPK* 1.3.1–7](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.3.1-7) on memory and recognition, the load-bearing argument that no series of distinct *jñānas* can sustain *pratyabhijñā* unless one persisting *jñātṛ* underlies them); the *Kriyā-adhikāra* shows that all action presupposes the same sovereign agent-consciousness ([*ĪPK* 2.1.1–8](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.1.1-8) on the unity of the agent across *kriyā*-moments, and [*ĪPK* 2.4.1–21](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.4.1-21) on *kriyā* itself as Śiva's intrinsic *śakti* rather than as a property added to *cit*); the *Āgama-adhikāra* coordinates the philosophical analysis with scriptural Trika ([*ĪPK* 3.1.1–9](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.1.1-9) and [*ĪPK* 3.2.1–20](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.1-20)); the *Tattva-saṃgraha* recapitulates the manifest world as *ābhāsa* projected by Śiva's *svātantrya* ([*ĪPK* 4.1.1–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.1.1-17)). The structural innovations — *ābhāsa-vāda* as a positive theory of manifestation ([*ĪPK* 2.3.1–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.3.1-17), distinguishing *ābhāsa* from Vedāntic *adhyāsa* in that the appearing is real *as* Śiva's free expression and not a sublatable error), *aham*-as-*pūrṇāhantā* ([*ĪPK* 1.5.13–14](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.13-14)), the recognition-structure of liberation ([*ĪPK* 4.1.16–17](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/4.1.16-17)) — set the agenda for all subsequent Pratyabhijñā literature, including Abhinavagupta's two *Vimarśinīs*.

engaged_works.title: Ajada-Pramatr-Siddhi
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: First text of the Siddhitrayī, composed in the same period as the Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the first treatise of the *Siddhitrayī* — the three short philosophical *prakaraṇas* that establish the foundations of Pratyabhijñā.
engaged_works.summary: The first of the three treatises constituting Utpaladeva's *Siddhitrayī* ("Triad of Establishments"), in approximately twenty-eight verses with the author's own *vṛtti*. The work establishes the *ajaḍatva* of the *pramātṛ* — the irreducibly non-inert (i.e., self-aware, conscious) character of the cognizing subject — against the Buddhist *jñāna-santāna* (cognition-stream) account that reduces the subject to a sequence of momentary cognitive events (*Ajaḍa-Pramātṛ-Siddhi* vv. 1–10, the foundational disposition). Utpaladeva's central argument is that no series of momentary cognitions, however conjoined, can constitute the unitary self-awareness presupposed by every act of recognition (*pratyabhijñā*) — "this is the same as what I saw before" (*APS* vv. 11–22, the regress argument against momentariness). The persistent self-aware subject, possessing intrinsic *vimarśa*, is therefore *ajaḍa*: not an inert substrate but an active reflexive consciousness (*APS* vv. 23–28, the constructive conclusion). This thesis is the load-bearing premise of the entire *Pratyabhijñā* programme: only an *ajaḍa-pramātṛ* can be the supreme Śiva-consciousness whose recognition constitutes liberation. The text is the analytic foundation that the *Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā* presupposes and elaborates (paralleled at [*ĪPK* 1.3.1–7](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.3.1-7) and [*ĪPK* 1.4.1–8](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.4.1-8)).

engaged_works.title: Sambandha-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; second treatise of the *Siddhitrayī*.
engaged_works.summary: The second treatise of the *Siddhitrayī*, in roughly twenty-three verses with auto-*vṛtti*, establishing the reality and analyzability of *sambandha* (relation) — most pivotally the relation of the *pramātṛ* to its objects, and the relation of cognition to its content (*Sambandha-Siddhi* vv. 1–8). Utpaladeva confronts the Dharmakīrtian dilemma that any real relation between two relata would have to itself stand in further relations to each, generating regress (*SamS* vv. 9–14, addressing Dharmakīrti's *Sambandha-Parīkṣā*); he argues that the regress is dissolved once relation is understood not as a third entity standing between independent relata but as a mode of the supreme self-aware *prakāśa* in which both relata are already manifest (*SamS* vv. 15–23). *Sambandha* is therefore intrinsic to *vimarśa* itself: consciousness's self-articulation as subject-related-to-object is not an addition to consciousness but its very form. The text supplies the relational metaphysics that makes *ābhāsa-vāda* possible — for *ābhāsas* (manifestations) to be coherent at all, they must be related within a single self-aware *prakāśa*, and the *Sambandha-Siddhi* shows this is logically tenable (the result presupposed at [*ĪPK* 2.3.1–7](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.3.1-7)).

engaged_works.title: Ishvara-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; third and culminating treatise of the *Siddhitrayī*.
engaged_works.summary: The third and culminating treatise of the *Siddhitrayī*, in roughly thirty-four verses with auto-*vṛtti*, establishing *Īśvara* — the supreme Lord, identified as Śiva-consciousness — by argued extension of the *ajaḍa-pramātṛ* and *sambandha* doctrines (*Īśvara-Siddhi* vv. 1–10, the foundational disposition). Utpaladeva argues that the unitary self-aware subject established in the first treatise, related to its world-content as established in the second, cannot be an arbitrarily individuated finite consciousness: the very structure of *vimarśa* — its *svātantrya* in projecting and recognizing its objects — requires that, at the limit, the subject be an unconditionally sovereign consciousness whose self-relating *aham* contains the entire manifestation (*ĪS* vv. 11–24). This is *Īśvara*: not a cosmic agent external to consciousness but consciousness in its own *pūrṇāhantā* (*ĪS* vv. 25–30). The bound *jīva* is then the same *Īśvara* under self-imposed *saṃkoca* (contraction) — the foundational claim (*ĪS* vv. 31–34) subsequently developed in the *Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā* ([*ĪPK* 3.2.5–10](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.5-10)) and consolidated in Kṣemarāja's *Pratyabhijñā-Hṛdaya* sūtra 4. The *Siddhitrayī* together constitutes the analytic propaedeutic to the school: subject established, relation established, supreme Subject established.

engaged_works.title: Shiva-Stotravali
engaged_works.summary: A collection of approximately twenty stotras to Śiva that converts the technical *Pratyabhijñā* framework into first-person devotional address. The stotras are not separate from the philosophy: they enact, in liturgical voice, the recognition (*pratyabhijñā*) that the worshipping *aham* is itself the supreme *aham* of Śiva, and that *bhakti* is the natural condition of a self that has recognized its own *svātantrya* ([*Śiva-Stotrāvalī* 1.1–4](cite://utpaladeva/shiva-stotravali/1.1-4), the opening *Bhakti-Stotra*). Recurring themes include the impossibility of a real distance between worshipper and worshipped within a single non-dual consciousness (*Stotrāvalī* 5.5–13, *Sarvātmadarśana-Stotra*), the *camatkāra* (wonder) that arises when the limited subject glimpses its own infinite ground (*Stotrāvalī* 13.1–18 on the *camatkāra*-register), and the doctrine that worship, agency, and grace are all modes of Śiva's own self-relating *vimarśa* (*Stotrāvalī* 16.10–22). Kṣemarāja's *Stava-Cintāmaṇi* commentary tradition treats the *Stotrāvalī* as the affective and soteriological complement of the *Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā*. The text is an important comparator for Vaiṣṇava devotional metaphysics, since it demonstrates that strong non-dualism need not abolish *bhakti* but can re-ground it in self-recognition.
